title: Bi-modal polymer networks: Composition-dependent trends in thermal, volumetric and structural properties from molecular dynamics simulation
abstract: Thermal and volumetric properties of mixed epoxy networks were characterized with molecular dynamics simulation. Atomistically detailed models of epoxy networks of diglycidyl ether of bisphenol (DGEBA) cured with stoichiometric binary mixtures of a flexible cross-linker poly(oxypropylene) diamine (POP) and a stiff cross-linker 4,4'-methylenebis(cyclohexylamine) (MCA), having molecular weights of 1987 and 210 g/mol respectively, were prepared. Epoxy networks formed by five different compositions of the cross-linkers ranging from pure POP to pure MCA were constructed, and a network topology analysis was carried out to verify that each network chain was connected to all other chains by a path of bonded molecules. The glass transition temperature (Tg), coefficient of volume thermal expansion (CVTE), heat capacity and thermal conductivity of these network structures were determined as a function of the network composition. The simulated values of these properties are compared with predictions from theories, empirical correlations and experiments from the literature. In general, it is observed that an increase in the mass fraction of MCA leads to an increase in the Tg and a decrease in the CVTE; furthermore, the breadth of the transition as exhibited by the change in the specific volume, CVTE, and heat capacity increases with an increase in the MCA content. The differences in the flexibility of the network components were analyzed using a number of quantitative measures. Using these results, a molecular mechanism is proposed for the observation of the network composition dependence of the breadth of the glass transition in these systems.
results: Following the same protocol as in our previous work, each of the mixed networks was subjected to stepwise cooling from a temperature (T) of 735 K to 150 K in steps of 15 K per 2.0 ns. The specific volume (y) was averaged over the final 1.0 ns of each temperature step. The glass transition regions of the networks presented suggest that the breadth of the glass transition increases with increasing values of x, i.e., with increasing MCA content. The computed Tg values increase with increasing MCA composition. The cooling rate used here is on the order of 10^9 K/s, or about 10 orders of magnitude faster than typical experimental cooling rates of ~ 10^{-1} K/s, thus the values of Tg from simulation are expected to be significantly higher than those from experiment.
discussion: At a given temperature, the volumetric and thermal properties of the mixed networks were sensitive to the cross-linker composition. Each of our systems had the same DGEBA monomer, thus we attribute the observed property changes to the differences in the molecular structure, i.e., to the differences in the length and stiffness of the two cross-linkers. The trends of cp(T) that we observe cannot be due to these effects, as they occur in the absence of crystallization during a cooling scan. Thus, the behavior of cp in the mixed networks is best explained as a result of decreasing heat capacity at high temperatures in the rubbery state. The flexibility of the crosslinker has a significant effect on the thermal and volumetric properties of the cross-linked network, especially on the breadth of the glass transition.
conclusion: We have built detailed atomistic models of cross-linked networks formed by the epoxy monomer DGEBA and a mixture of two cross-linkers, a flexible POP and a stiff MCA. Mixed networks of five compositions were prepared by varying the relative amounts of POP and MCA. An analysis of the network topology was performed to ensure that no local clusters exist, i.e., that all network junctions in the system were connected by a path spanning bonded molecules. The model structures were analyzed to determine a number of volumetric and thermal properties as a function of the network composition: volume-temperature behavior across the glass transition, the glass transition temperature, the coefficient of volume thermal expansion, the heat capacity and the thermal conductivity. Our results show that the flexibility of the crosslinker has a significant effect on the thermal and volumetric properties of the cross-linked network, especially on the breadth of the glass transition.
